headline: GRAINS DRIFT AS IRAN PREMIUM UNWINDS
subheadline: Crude pulls back on Lebanon ceasefire progress while Belt weather stays cooperative.
lead: Corn closed $4.41, up a quarter-cent on nothing worth mentioning. The real story is crude's pullback from yesterday's Iran spike-WTI gave back 72 cents to $90.74 as Trump's Lebanon diplomacy cooled the geopolitical premium. Grains are following energy's lead lower, but the moves are thin, managed money still sorting out whether yesterday's 5% crude explosion was signal or noise.
the_takeaway: Energy drives grain sentiment more than weather right now.
teaser: Iran premium unwinds as diplomatic progress cools commodity markets.

one_number.unit: cents crude gave back from yesterday's spike
one_number.context: Trump's Lebanon ceasefire progress pulled crude off multi-week highs, dragging grain sentiment with it. The 72-cent reversal shows how fast geopolitical premiums can unwind when diplomacy works. Grains are still following energy's narrative more than their own fundamentals.

yesterdays_call.summary: Called geopolitical premium back after three weeks of unwinding.
yesterdays_call.note: The premium came and went in 24 hours, diplomacy reset the story.

sections.title: Grains Follow Energy Lower
sections.body: Corn managed a quarter-cent gain to $4.42 but beans slipped a penny and a half to $11.78. **The action is thin, driven more by crude's 72-cent pullback than anything happening in the fields.** Welcome rains hit parts of the Plains overnight while Belt conditions stay cooperative for early development. December corn held flat at $4.70, telling you the front months are trading energy correlation, not crop fundamentals. Funds are still digesting yesterday's Iran spike, no conviction either direction.
sections.catalyst: Crude oil pullback on Trump Lebanon ceasefire progress
sections.bottom_line: Energy drives grain sentiment, not weather yet.
sections.vs_yesterday: Grains: Iran premium unwind exactly as fast as it built.

sections.title: Cattle Rally Extends
sections.body: Live cattle added another 60 cents to $240.40, **the third straight session of steady gains despite no change in the fed cattle trade.** Feeders led higher, up $2.80 to $351.18, as grass conditions improve across the Plains. The rally looks technical-shorts covering after last week's breakdown attempt failed. Cash trade hasn't reset since the Memorial Day break, but packers aren't pushing lower either. Box beef cutout steady, no new catalyst driving this move.
sections.bottom_line: Rally on short covering, no fundamental driver yet.
sections.vs_yesterday: Cattle: momentum building exactly as called Monday.

outside_the_pit.title: USTR Issues Section 301 Determination for Brazil
outside_the_pit.body: Trade Representative launches formal review of Brazilian trade practices affecting US ag exports. The probe targets intellectual property and market access barriers that have cost US farmers billions in lost sales opportunities.

outside_the_pit.title: FTC Confirms Fertilizer Price Investigation
outside_the_pit.body: Federal antitrust investigators are probing rising fertilizer costs that have squeezed farm margins nationwide. The investigation targets potential price coordination among major suppliers during the post-pandemic input cost surge.

outside_the_pit.title: New World Screwworm Found Near US Border
outside_the_pit.body: Devastating livestock parasite detected just 31 miles from the border could threaten US cattle industry. The fly eats warm-blooded animals alive and represents millions in potential economic damage if it crosses into domestic herds.

spread_to_watch.label: December corn / July corn carry
spread_to_watch.commentary: The carry's not collapsing despite cooperative weather because old-crop supplies are still tight. **When new-crop starts pricing weather instead of energy correlation, this spread tells the story first.** Watch for compression if Belt conditions stay ideal through pollination.

basis.headline: Eastern Belt firming on ethanol demand recovery
basis.body: Corn basis east of the Mississippi is **adding 2-3 cents** as ethanol plants return from maintenance shutdowns. Western Belt staying soft, consistent with the seasonal as river logistics improve. **The divergence says old-crop supplies are tighter than the futures alone suggest.** Producers with on-farm storage have a window the board isn't fully pricing.

weekly_thread.question: Will the grain liquidation finish before weather takes control of the story?
weekly_thread.status_text: The liquidation paused as energy volatility grabbed the steering wheel. Grains are following crude's lead, not their own fundamentals, which suggests the fund positioning reset isn't finished yet.

the_more_you_know.title: Why crude's 72-cent reversal matters for grain traders
the_more_you_know.body: Yesterday's 5% crude spike and today's 72-cent pullback show how fast geopolitical premiums can build and collapse in modern markets. **Grains followed both moves despite no change in Belt weather or USDA data.** The correlation runs through the energy input cost channel-higher crude means higher diesel, nitrogen, and transport costs, which eventually shows up in planted acreage decisions. When energy swings 5% in a day, grain traders price the tail risk even when the fundamentals haven't changed. The speed of today's reversal tells you Trump's diplomacy works faster than the old playbook assumed.

watch_list.desc: USDA weekly export sales; soybeans under 400K MT keeps chart in control
Fed cattle trade reset; packers bidding $245 or higher breaks the range
December corn below $4.65 signals new-crop weather premium building

daily_quote.text: Lost time is never found again.

source_summary: CME Group, USDA, energy markets, weather services
